A transfer (slider) board and the assistance of three people

studentQuestionArray.content: A client is being transferred to the nursing unit from the postanesthesia care unit after spinal fusion with rod insertion to treat spinal instability from severe arthritis. The nurse would prepare to transfer the client from the stretcher to the bed by using which best method?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
After spinal fusion, with or without instrumentation, the client is transferred from the stretcher to the bed using a transfer (slider) board and the assistance of three people, with one at the head to protect or support the client's head and neck. This strategy permits optimal stabilization and support of the spine while allowing the client to be moved smoothly and gently.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Note the strategic word, best. Focus on the subject, care of the client following spinal fusion. This question can be answered by analyzing the level of comfort and stability provided to the client's spine with the amounts of assistance given in each option. Using this approach, you can eliminate each of the incorrect options.

"I would try not to remain in the same position for a long period of time."
"I need to use large joints instead of small joints when performing activities."
"Pain or fatigue is expected, and I would try to continue with the activity if this occurs."

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has provided instructions to a client with a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis about measures to protect the joints. Which statement by the client indicates a need for further instruction?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The client needs to be instructed to use pain or fatigue as an indicator and guide to increase, maintain, or decrease an activity level. If pain or fatigue is experienced, the client needs to rest. The client needs to learn to slide objects rather than lifting them and not remain in the same position for a long time. Whenever possible, the client would use large joints instead of small joints for activities and would use the joints in their most natural position.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Note the strategic words, need for further instructions. This phrasing indicates a negative event query and asks you to select an option that is an incorrect statement. Focus on the statement by the client that identifies an activity that would place stress or pressure on the body and joints.

Anorexia

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reviewing the health care record of a client with a new diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The nurse understands that which is an early clinical manifestation of RA?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic, progressive, systemic inflammatory autoimmune disease process that affects primarily the synovial joints. Early clinical manifestations of RA include complaints of fatigue, generalized weakness, anorexia, and weight loss. Anemia, amenorrhea, and night sweats are not early manifestations of RA.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Note the strategic word, early, to assist in directing you to the correct option. Think about the pathophysiology and manifestations of RA. Recall that fatigue, generalized weakness, anorexia, and weight loss are early manifestations.

optionList.content: Age of onset is generally 65 years of age or older

Systemic symptoms such as fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss
Joint pain is asymmetrical and associated with past injuries to the joint
Age of onset is generally 65 years of age or older
Complaint of pain that is more severe after activity

Joint pain is asymmetrical and associated with past injuries to the joint

studentQuestionArray.content: The clinic nurse is performing an assessment on a client with a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The nurse checks for which assessment finding that is associated with RA?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
In clients diagnosed with RA, systemic symptoms such as fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, and nonspecific aching and stiffness may appear before joint manifestations. RA is characterized by chronic joint pain of variable intensity, which is more severe on rising in the morning. The age of onset for RA is most commonly between 30 and 50 years of age. A complaint of pain that is more severe after activity and asymmetrical joint pain associated with past injuries to the joint are more commonly seen in osteoarthritis.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, assessment findings associated with RA. Think about the pathophysiology associated with rheumatoid arthritis. Also, knowledge of the difference between RA and osteoarthritis will assist you in eliminating the remaining options.

studentQuestionArray.content: An older client with rheumatoid arthritis has been instructed by the primary health care provider to take ibuprofen 400 mg orally (PO) three times daily. The home care nurse reading the medication prescription knows that the instruction has been effective when the client states the instructed dose is which?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
For acute or chronic rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis, the normal oral adult dose for an older client is 400 to 800 mg three or four times daily. The other options are incorrect.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Note the strategic word, effective. Focus on the subject, proper dosing for ibuprofen. Knowledge of the normal dosage for ibuprofen is required to answer this question. Noting the diagnosis of the client in the question and that the client is an older client will assist in directing you to the correct option.

optionList.content: Allergic

Allergic

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse provides instructions to a client with bilateral deformities of the joints of the fingers due to rheumatoid arthritis. When providing teaching about the disease process, the nurse would inform the client that the changes are most likely due to what type of response?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The most likely cause for rheumatoid arthritis is activation of an autoimmune response. This is thought to trigger antigen-antibody responses and release of lysosomes from phagocytic cells, which ultimately attack the cartilage and synovia, with resultant synovitis. Other theories related to the cause of rheumatoid arthritis have been proposed, but the most likely cause is an autoimmune reaction.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Note the strategic words, most likely. Recalling the mechanisms related to joint damage in clients with rheumatoid arthritis will direct you to the correct option. Remember that the most likely cause for rheumatoid arthritis is activation of an autoimmune response.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has completed giving discharge instructions to a client with osteoarthritis who has had a total joint replacement (TJR) of the knee with a metal prosthetic system. The nurse determines that the client understands the instructions if the client makes which statement?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
A TJR is also known as a total joint arthroplasty (TJA). The client must inform other caregivers of the presence of the metal implant because certain tests and procedures will need to be avoided. After total knee replacement, the client needs to report signs and symptoms of infection and any changes in the shape of the knee. These could indicate developing complications. With a metal implant, the client may be on anticoagulant therapy and needs to report adverse effects of this therapy, including bleeding from a variety of sources, and the client will need antibiotic prophylaxis for invasive procedures.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, client teaching points after total knee replacement. The question refers to a metal prosthesis, which indicates that anticoagulant therapy is indicated. Therefore, eliminate options 1 and 4. It is important to report signs and symptoms of infection, so option 2 also is eliminated.

optionList.content: Renal colic
Hypertension
Diabetes mellitus
Gastric ulceration
Renal colic

studentQuestionArray.content: The primary health care provider (PHCP) writes a prescription for acetylsalicylic acid, or aspirin, for a client who was admitted to the hospital with joint pain from rheumatoid arthritis. The nurse contacts the PHCP to verify the prescription if which finding is noted in the assessment data?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Acetylsalicylic acid is a nonsteroidal agent that is prescribed for its anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and anticoagulant properties. Contraindications to the medication include gastrointestinal bleeding or ulceration, bleeding disorders, history of hypersensitivity to aspirin or other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications, impaired hepatic function, and chicken pox or flu in children or teenagers. The items noted in options 1, 2, and 3 are not contraindications to this medication.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, the contraindications associated with acetylsalicylic acid. Focusing on the name of the medication, acetylsalicylic acid, will assist in directing you to the correct option.

studentQuestionArray.content: A fluorescent antinuclear antibody titer (FANA) is performed in a client suspected of having rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Which laboratory value is most consistent with RA?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:

Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic, progressive, systemic inflammatory autoimmune disease process that affects primarily the synovial joints. The antinuclear antibody test measures the titer of antibodies that destroy the nuclei cells and cause tissue death. When the fluorescent method is used, the test sometimes is referred to as FANA. If this test is positive, a value greater than 1:8 will be present. Therefore, the options below this value are incorrect.


Test-Taking Strategy:

Focus on the subject, a positive FANA result. Note the strategic word, most. Knowledge of the FANA and the normal value is required to answer this question. In this situation, because the test is diagnosing RA, it is best to select the highest titer.

optionList.content: Administer an analgesic.
Immobilize the knee temporarily.
Notify the primary health care provider.
Put the client's knee through full passive range of motion.
Administer an analgesic.
Immobilize the knee temporarily.
Notify the primary health care provider.
Put the client's knee through full passive range of motion.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with rheumatoid arthritis who had a total knee arthroplasty tells the nurse that there is pain with extension of the knee. The nurse would perform which action?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Pain with knee extension is a common complaint of clients after knee arthroplasty; therefore, administering an analgesic would be the appropriate action. Immobilizing the knee will not help. The pain may be the result of a flexion contracture that developed preoperatively as the client tried to reduce the pain by keeping the knee partially flexed much of the time. The nurse needs to encourage the client to keep the knee extended and administer analgesics as needed. Pain is expected postoperatively, so there is no need to notify the primary health care provider based on the symptom described. Full passive range of motion can be harmful to the knee replacement.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, care of the client following total knee replacement. The question states that there is pain with extension. Immobilizing the knee will not help, so this option may be eliminated first. Putting the joint through full range of motion may be more than the client can tolerate; also, the client usually has a continuous passive motion machine in place that controls the amount of flexion and extension of the joint. Regarding the remaining options, recall that a flexion contracture can occur preoperatively, which would lead you to choose medicating the client rather than notifying the primary health care provider.

Joint pain that diminishes after rest
Elevated antinuclear antibody levels
Joint pain that intensifies with activity
Elevated white blood cell count
A decreased sedimentation rate

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client with osteoarthritis. The nurse performs an assessment knowing that which clinical manifestations are associated with the disorder? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The stiffness and joint pain that occur in osteoarthritis diminish after rest and intensify with activity. No specific laboratory findings are useful in diagnosing osteoarthritis. The client may have a normal or slightly elevated sedimentation rate. Morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes occurs in rheumatoid arthritis. Elevated white blood cell counts, platelet counts, and antinuclear antibody levels occur in rheumatoid arthritis.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Focus on the subject, clinical manifestations of osteoarthritis. Use knowledge about the differences between osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis to answer this question. Note that the incorrect options address laboratory values used for rheumatoid arthritis. Also recall that the stiffness and joint pain that occur in osteoarthritis diminish after rest and intensify with activity.

optionList.content: Fatigue
Weight gain

Morning stiffness
Pain with movement only
Fatigue
Weight gain
Restlessness
Morning stiffness
Pain with movement only

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is gathering subjective and objective data from a client with a diagnosis of suspected rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The nurse would expect to note which early signs and symptoms of RA? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Early signs and symptoms of RA include fatigue, weight loss, fever, malaise, morning stiffness, pain at rest and with movement, and complaints of night pain. The involved joints appear edematous.

Test-Taking Strategy:
Note the strategic word, early, and focus on the subject, signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis. Eliminate option 5 because of the closed-ended word "only." Thinking about the pathophysiology associated with this diagnosis will assist in eliminating the options of weight gain and restlessness.
